Aetna ID: W292798278
Incident
Motor vehicle vs. pedestrian — Saturday, April 4, 2026 · 10:28 AM

Carolyn was struck by a vehicle while pumping gas at the Jacksons Shell station, 10801 NE 68th St, Kirkland, WA. She was standing outside her car on the driver's side, making funny faces at Mira through the rear window. Mira was buckled in a front-facing car seat in the rear driver's side seat — the same side as the impact. Carolyn stated she never saw the striking vehicle. She sustained critical polytrauma and was transported emergently to Harborview Medical Center by Kirkland Fire & Rescue, who arrived at 10:35 AM. Mira was checked by Aid on scene and had no injuries. Kevin Carlberg arrived on scene and took custody of Mira.

Official report: Kirkland PD Case #26-12446, Report No. EG92010. Investigating officer: Ofc. Huebner, Cameron (Badge 652). Approved by: DesCamp, Jacob (643). Body-worn camera footage and all scene photographs uploaded to Evidence.com.

Unit 1 — Striking Vehicle
2017 Toyota Avalon · WA BJN6096
Driver: On Yuet Wong, DOB 06/22/1949 (age 76 at time of incident)
Address: 11310 NE 117th Pl, Kirkland, WA 98034
License: WDL7S253433B (WA) · VIN: 4T1BK1EB3HU264851
Registered owner: Wong, TSZ K
Insurance: Progressive #948631281

Wong's statement: She was heading westbound on NE 68th St, stopped to make a left turn into the Shell parking lot, then stated her car "malfunctioned," she had "no brakes," and was unable to control the vehicle.

Video evidence contradicts this: Officer Biddle reviewed footage showing Unit 1 appearing to accelerate into the parking lot, with no attempts at braking, driving directly into Unit 2. Wong's brake-failure claim is not supported by the video record.

Sobriety: HGN test — no nystagmus detected. Breath test (PBT): 0.000. No alcohol or drug involvement.
Unit 2 — Victim Vehicle
2013 Toyota Prius · WA BRU3454
Driver: Carolyn R. Carlberg, DOB 01/01/1985
Address: 10410 NE 52nd St, Kirkland, WA 98033
License: WDL5762JD53B (WA) · VIN: JTDKN3DU8D0355514
Insurance: Progressive #931521565
Injury class: 5 (most severe) · Nature: Head/Hip/Legs
Medically transported: Yes — Harborview Medical Center
Vehicle towed by Macs Towing (impounded)

Carolyn's statement (at Harborview): Alert and talking when officer arrived. Unable to physically sign medical release form due to injuries — gave verbal consent on body cam. Stated she never saw Unit 1. Was pumping gas and making funny faces at Mira when she was suddenly struck.
Passenger — Unit 2
Mira L. Carlberg · DOB 11/24/2019
Age 6 at time of incident
Seat position 7 — rear driver's side (same side as impact)
Restraint: front-facing child safety seat
Injury class: 1 (minor/possible)
Checked by Aid on scene — no injuries found

Citations Issued
Wong cited under two statutes
RCW 46.30.020 — Operating a Motor Vehicle Without Insurance
Note: Wong provided valid insurance approximately one hour after citation was issued.

RCW 46.61.400 — Speed Too Fast For Conditions

Both charges filed under Citation #6A0165290 (one citation number covering two charges, as documented in the police report).

Wong's vehicle was an owner-requested tow, also handled by Macs Towing.
Accident Scene Documentation
Scene analysis — Jacksons Shell, 10801 NE 68th St, Kirkland · April 4, 2026 · 10:28 AM

Carolyn's silver Toyota Prius (WA plate BRU3454) was stationary at pumps 1/2 of the Jacksons Shell station. She was standing on the driver's side pumping gas, facing the rear window making funny faces at Mira, who was buckled into a front-facing child safety seat in the rear driver's side seat. A white 2017 Toyota Avalon (WA plate BJN6096), driven by On Yuet Wong (age 76), entered the parking lot from NE 68th St and struck the Prius at the driver's side rear quarter panel without braking. The gas pump was completely sheared from its concrete-anchored base.

Per the police diagram (page 6 of report EG92010): Unit 1 entered from NE 68th St traveling diagonally across the parking lot at speed and struck Unit 2 at the pump island. The trajectory shown is consistent with the impact geometry visible in the scene photographs. Officer Biddle reviewed video footage and confirmed Unit 1 was accelerating with no braking attempts before impact.

Carolyn's position at impact: She was physically occupying the space between the pump and the driver's side of the car — the exact compression zone of the collision. She was not struck while standing nearby. She was in the direct impact corridor between two colliding structures at the moment of contact. Carolyn confirmed at Harborview she never saw the striking vehicle.

Official Police Diagram — Report EG92010, Page 6 of 6 · Officer Huebner #652
Trajectory of Unit 1 (red, Wong's Avalon): Entered from NE 68th St traveling southeast, crossed the parking lot diagonally at speed, and struck Unit 2 (blue, Carolyn's Prius) at the pump island. Yellow arrows show the vehicle's path. The diagram confirms there was no evasive action or lane correction before impact — the vehicle traveled in a straight accelerating line from the street directly to the point of impact. Unit 2 driver is shown at the pump ("Unit 2 Driver Pumping Gas"). Point of impact is at the gas pump island where Carolyn was standing. Drawing not to scale.
Photo 1 — Driver's side impact zone. Pump completely sheared and lying on its side. Significant lateral crushing of the Prius rear quarter panel and door. Bollards displaced.
Photo 2 — Rear of Prius (BRU3454). Rear quarter and bumper damage visible. Pump sheared completely off base, lying displaced with hoses intact. Debris scatter consistent with high-energy impact.
Photo 3 — Striking vehicle (white Toyota Avalon) embedded into driver's side of Prius. Avalon front end substantially crumpled. Both vehicles displaced from original positions. Kirkland Fire & Rescue visible in background.
Photo 4 — Close-up of impact interface. Avalon front-end crush depth approximately 12–18 inches. Deep lateral intrusion into Prius driver's side. Both vehicles exhibit structural deformation consistent with high-speed impact.
Estimated impact speed
35–45 mph, consistent with the ~35 mph reported in the medical record and with the citation issued for "Speed Too Fast For Conditions" (RCW 46.61.400). Wong was cited under this statute, implying the investigating officer determined speed was a causative factor. Video reviewed by Officer Biddle confirmed Unit 1 was accelerating with no braking attempts. Physical evidence: (1) Gas pump completely sheared from concrete-anchored base; (2) Avalon front-end crush depth of ~12–18 inches; (3) Deep lateral intrusion into Prius body — not surface deformation; (4) Debris scatter and vehicle displacement. Below ~25 mph, this damage profile does not occur.
Vehicles involved
Unit 2 (victim): Silver 2013 Toyota Prius HB, WA plate BRU3454, VIN JTDKN3DU8D0355514, Thule roof box fitted. Owned and driven by Carolyn R. Carlberg. Unit 1 (striking): White 2017 Toyota Avalon SD, WA plate BJN6096, VIN 4T1BK1EB3HU264851. Driven by On Yuet Wong (age 76). Registered owner: Wong, TSZ K. Both vehicles towed by Macs Towing.
Impact geometry
The Avalon struck the driver's side rear quarter panel of the stationary Prius — a lateral impact at the exact location where Carolyn was standing. The force vector drove the Prius laterally into the pump structure, which sheared from its mount. Carolyn was in the direct compression zone between the striking vehicle and the pump at the moment of impact.
Mira's position
Mira L. Carlberg (DOB 11/24/2019) was in seat position 7 — rear driver's side — buckled into a front-facing child safety seat. This is the same side as the impact. The rear door and quarter panel show significant deformation. Mira was checked by Aid on scene and had no injuries (injury class 1 — minor/possible). Her survival uninjured is consistent with the child safety seat providing restraint and the impact force being substantially absorbed by the pump, bollards, and vehicle sheet metal before transmitting to the cabin. She was released to her father, Kevin Carlberg, who arrived at the scene.
Scene responders
Kirkland Fire & Rescue visible on scene in photos 1 and 3. Washington state jurisdiction — Kirkland, WA.
Injuries
Pelvis — Primary Injury
Multiple unstable vertical shear fractures
Bilateral obturator ring fractures, bilateral pubic rami (left extending into symphysis, right acetabular wall), left sacral ala fractures (zones 1–2), left SI joint disruption with superior iliac subluxation. Mechanism: LC2 lateral compression with vertical shear. Bilateral hematoma (pelvic sidewall, retroperitoneal, presacral).
Sacrum / Lumbar Spine
Sacral fracture + L5–S1 disruption
Left posterior pelvic ring fractures extending into left S1 neuroforamen. S3/S4 minimally displaced transverse fracture. S3 buckle fracture. New L5 and S1 neurological deficits detected April 8, prompting urgent spinal fusion surgery.
Right Ankle
Lateral malleolus avulsion fracture
Closed avulsion fracture of the right lateral malleolus (fibula), mildly displaced with overlying soft tissue swelling. Alignment normal on non-weightbearing imaging. Tibia intact. Right foot X-ray normal.
Maxilla / Upper Jaw
Alveolar fracture — maxillary incisors
Acute mildly displaced alveolar fracture involving the anterior nasal spine. Periapical lucencies at bilateral frontal maxillary incisors and right lateral maxillary incisor, indicating dental trauma. Treated with oral/maxillofacial surgery. No orbital or skull base fracture.
Scalp
Left temporal laceration
1 cm laceration, left temporal scalp. Left frontoparietal hematoma without underlying fracture. CT head: no intracranial hemorrhage, no infarct, no mass. Brain parenchyma normal. Repaired with 2 sutures day of admission.
Vascular / Soft Tissue
Pelvic & retroperitoneal hematomas
Left pelvic sidewall hematoma, retroperitoneal hematoma along psoas, presacral hemoperitoneum, space of Retzius fluid. CT cystogram: no bladder extravasation. No active arterial bleeding on CTA. Hematoma worsened on same-day CT but stabilized post-fixation.
Surgical Procedures
1
Apr 4
2026
Emergency scalp laceration repair
Local anesthesia (lidocaine 1% without epi) · 1 cm left temporal scalp wound · 2 simple interrupted sutures (5-0 fast-absorbing gut) · wound explored through full depth — no fracture, no vascular damage · tolerated well, no complications. Performed by Dr. V. Koski-Karell, authorized by Dr. Ross Kessler.
2
Apr 4–5
2026
Left femoral skeletal traction (supracondylar traction pin)
Horizontal traction pin placed through the supracondylar left femur to stabilize the vertically unstable pelvis fracture, reduce deformity, and control pain pending definitive fixation. Confirmed by post-procedure knee X-ray taken at 4:33 PM on April 4, which shows the traction pin already in place. The nursing lines record documents formal placement at April 5, 3:42 AM — likely reflecting delayed documentation in a high-acuity setting rather than the actual placement time. Traction removed April 6 at 15:01 in preparation for open reduction internal fixation. This is standard bridging management for high-energy pelvic ring injuries.
3
Apr 4–6
2026
Oral / maxillofacial surgery — alveolar fracture repair
Surgical management of acute mildly displaced alveolar fracture of the anterior nasal spine with periapical involvement of bilateral frontal maxillary incisors and right lateral maxillary incisor, as documented on CT maxillofacial. Procedure performed by oral/maxillofacial surgery service. Exact operative note not included in shared records — confirm with OMFS team for full documentation.
4
Apr 6
2026
Open reduction internal fixation — pelvis posterior ring + anterior external fixator
Duration: Anesthesia 11:23–15:44 (4 hrs 21 min) · Surgeons: Dr. Conor P. Kleweno (orthopaedic) · Anesthesia: Dr. Michael Kaplan / CRNA Kimberly Terrill

Procedures performed: open reduction and internal fixation of the posterior pelvic ring with plate and screw construct; application of anterior external fixation device; left sacroiliac joint fixation with transfixing screw.

Blood products: 3× packed RBC transfusions + 1× fresh frozen plasma · Agents: Tranexamic acid (1,262.5 mg total), calcium chloride (2,000 mg), phenylephrine infusion · Fluids: 4,100 mL (LR + Plasma-Lyte) · Surgical drain placed left lateral hip (removed April 9).
5
Apr 8
2026
L5–ilium percutaneous posterior spinal fusion
Duration: Anesthesia 08:31–10:52 (2 hrs 21 min) · Surgeon: Dr. Haitao Zhou (spine) · Anesthesia: Dr. Yulia Obelcz / CRNA Sandy Corliss

Indication: New neurological deficits at L5 and S1 detected on morning of April 8, indicating nerve root compromise from sacral fracture fragments. Pre-op CT confirmed fractures extending into left S1 neuroforamen. Urgent minimally invasive posterior instrumented fusion L5–ilium performed.

Implant system: Globus Medical CREO MIS — 2× polyaxial tulip screws (30mm), 1× robotic modular 6.5×45mm screw, 2× lock caps, 1× 5.5mm×80mm curved titanium rod. Blood: 1× whole blood transfusion · Fluids: 600 mL. Post-op CT confirmed hardware in good position, no complication.

Lab Trends — Hemoglobin Recovery (Key Indicator)
Normal range for females: Hgb 11.5–15.5 g/dL · Hct 36–45% · Platelets 150–400 · WBC 4.3–10.0 · Calcium 8.9–10.2 mg/dL. Hemoglobin remains persistently low due to major blood loss from trauma and multiple surgeries, being gradually corrected by transfusions. Trending in right direction.

Troponin ×3 — all normal. Cardiac echo: preserved EF 57%, no effusion, no valve pathology. ECG normalized (early T-wave changes resolved — likely stress response). Candida auris PCR — negative. MRSA surveillance — negative. Coagulation (INR/PTT) — normal throughout.

Implanted Hardware

All hardware confirmed in good position on post-operative CT scans. No hardware complication. Anterior external fixator remains in place. Surgical drains removed April 9.

Additional Clinical Findings
Items below were confirmed in the medical record and are clinically significant. They are documented here to ensure nothing is lost across the care continuum.
Documentation gap — oral/maxillofacial surgery
The formal procedures list in this health summary contains seven documented procedures and does not include oral or maxillofacial surgery. Kevin Carlberg has confirmed this procedure was performed. It was likely performed by a consulting OMFS service whose operative note was not captured in this health summary document. Action required: request the full operative note from the OMFS/dental service at Harborview separately. Without it, the procedure is documented as family-reported only within this record.
Purple DTI — sacrum
A purple deep tissue injury (DTI) at the sacrum was documented by nursing on April 5 and classified as traumatic in origin. A purple DTI is a deep tissue pressure/crush injury — intact skin over a bony prominence with a purple or maroon discoloration indicating damaged underlying soft tissue. Given the sacral fractures directly beneath, this likely reflects direct crush trauma from the impact mechanism rather than pressure from positioning. Requires ongoing wound monitoring and skin care to prevent progression to open wound, particularly with prolonged bed rest and sacral hardware in place.
Gross hematuria on admission
Urinalysis on April 4 showed orange/cloudy urine with 3+ occult blood and an estimated 2,111 red blood cells per high-power field (normal: negative to ≤2). This is macroscopic/gross hematuria reflecting severe pelvic vascular disruption. It directly triggered the CT cystogram, which confirmed no bladder rupture or extravasation. The finding is consistent with the pelvic fracture pattern and retroperitoneal hematoma. Expected to resolve as pelvic hematoma stabilizes. A Foley catheter was placed on April 4 (13:21) for continuous monitoring and remains confirmed in place on post-operative CTs through April 8.
Pregnancy test — negative
Serum quantitative HCG drawn on arrival April 4: <1 mIU/mL (negative). This was a mandatory clinical decision point on admission — a positive result would have significantly altered imaging protocols (radiation minimization), medication choices, and surgical planning. Confirmed negative, allowing full trauma panscan and unrestricted pharmacological management.
Admission drug screen
Fentanyl: positive. Acetaminophen: positive. All other substances negative including alcohol (ethanol negative). Both positive results are consistent with standard EMS pain management administered en route — fentanyl is a first-line pre-hospital analgesic for major trauma; acetaminophen is routine. These are not toxicological concerns. The drug screen established a clean baseline and ruled out substance involvement as a contributing factor to the incident.
Cardiac wall motion abnormality
The April 5 echocardiogram identified hypokinesis of the basal-mid inferolateral and basal inferior cardiac segments on some views. This was an active clinical concern requiring workup. The cardiology interpretation noted the finding was inconsistent across views, raising the possibility of transient wall motion abnormality versus off-axis imaging artifact rather than primary myocardial infarction. Three serial troponin measurements (April 5 ×2, April 5 late) all returned normal, effectively ruling out significant cardiac muscle damage. Conclusion: likely demand ischemia or artifact from a heart under severe oxygen stress, not a primary cardiac event. ECG T-wave changes also normalized. No ongoing cardiac intervention required, but this was a genuine concurrent workup during the acute phase.
Hepatic function — normal
Hepatic function panel drawn April 4: albumin 3.9, total protein 6.3, total bilirubin 0.4, direct bilirubin 0.1, alkaline phosphatase 36, AST 27, ALT 19 — all within normal limits. Clinically significant because five blood transfusions, large-volume fluid resuscitation, calcium chloride (2,000 mg), and tranexamic acid all place metabolic and clearance demands on the liver. Normal function confirmed the liver was uninjured and capable of handling the acute resuscitation load.
Left thyroid nodule
1.8 cm nodule incidentally found on CTA neck/chest April 4. Nonemergent thyroid ultrasound recommended post-recovery for full characterization.
Chronic L5 spondylolysis
Bilateral, likely pre-existing. Grade 1 L5-on-S1 anterolisthesis. L5 limbus vertebra (incidental, well-corticated). Unfused L5 spinous process. Pre-existing condition, not acute — but relevant context for spinal recovery trajectory.
Splenic hypodensity
8 mm finding on CT abdomen — likely benign cyst or hemangioma. No splenic laceration. Non-urgent, reassess at follow-up imaging.
Left maxillary sinus cyst
Mucus retention cyst on maxillofacial CT. Incidental, non-urgent.
RDW-CV mildly elevated
14.6–14.7% (normal ≤14.5%) on recent CBCs — red cell size variation consistent with post-hemorrhagic anemia. Will resolve as hemoglobin recovers. Not a separate diagnosis.
Important negative findings
No pneumothorax · No aortic injury · No liver laceration · No bladder rupture (CT cystogram negative) · No intracranial hemorrhage · No cervical spine injury · No femoral fracture · No knee fractures bilaterally · No right foot fracture · No active arterial bleeding on CTA · No MRSA · No Candida auris · Alcohol negative · Troponin ×3 normal · Coagulation (INR/PTT) normal throughout.
Life Threat Assessment
This was an acutely life-threatening event. Carolyn was at genuine risk of death on April 4 and April 6.

The following is a clinical assessment of the severity of this event, grounded directly in the laboratory findings, imaging, and treatment decisions documented in the medical record.

Hemorrhagic shock — the primary mortal threat

Carolyn's hemoglobin reached a nadir of 6.8 g/dL on April 6, with a hematocrit of 19% — roughly half the normal red blood cell mass. Below approximately 7 g/dL, the heart can no longer compensate for oxygen deprivation, and cardiac arrest becomes an immediate risk. Her platelet count simultaneously fell to 104,000, meaning her blood's ability to clot was degrading. This combination — plummeting hemoglobin, dropping platelets, and worsening hematoma on serial CT scans taken the same day — is the beginning of hemorrhagic shock with consumptive coagulopathy. Without rapid surgical intervention and blood product resuscitation, this spiral is frequently unsurvivable. Her elevated venous lactate of 2.8 mmol/L on April 4, hours after arrival, confirmed she was already in systemic shock: her tissues were not receiving adequate oxygen.

The pelvic fracture pattern itself carries high mortality

Vertical shear pelvic fractures with posterior ring disruption and sacroiliac joint dislocation — the exact pattern Carolyn sustained — are among the highest-lethality injuries in emergency trauma medicine. The pelvis sits adjacent to the iliac arteries and veins. A posterior ring fracture disrupts a space with no natural tamponade: bleeding can expand freely into the retroperitoneum. Carolyn's CT at 8:46 PM on April 4 showed the hematoma had worsened compared to the scan taken hours earlier the same day. This was an actively expanding bleed at the time of the first surgeries.

The intraoperative pharmacology confirms how close to the edge she was

The drugs administered during her April 6 pelvic surgery tell the story precisely. Tranexamic acid is given specifically to prevent clotting collapse in massive hemorrhage — it is a last-resort hemostatic agent used when the coagulation system is failing. Calcium chloride (2,000 mg) was given to counteract the cardiac-depressant effect of rapid large-volume blood transfusions. Phenylephrine infusion is a vasopressor — it was required to maintain her blood pressure throughout the procedure. These are not precautionary measures. They are the pharmacological response to a patient in crisis.

The spinal and cardiac threats were concurrent

While hemorrhage was the most immediate threat, two additional life-altering risks were developing in parallel. New L5 and S1 neurological deficits appeared by April 8, indicating nerve root compression that — if untreated within hours — can become permanent. The team performed emergency spinal fusion the same morning. Separately, early ECGs showed T-wave inversions and the echocardiogram detected subtle wall motion irregularities, requiring serial troponins and cardiac monitoring to rule out a primary cardiac event in the setting of a heart under severe oxygen stress.

Why she survived

Harborview Medical Center is a nationally ranked Level 1 trauma center with particular expertise in high-energy pelvic trauma. The speed of the traction pin placement to stabilize the fracture and reduce displacement, the urgency of the April 6 ORIF, the blood product management (5 total transfusions across the admission), and the same-day decision to proceed with spinal fusion on April 8 all reflect expert trauma care executed at a high level. Dr. Kleweno is a specialist in complex pelvic reconstruction. Carolyn's youth, fitness, and absence of comorbidities gave her the physiological reserve to survive the acute phase. Both factors — exceptional care and exceptional physical constitution — were necessary. This outcome was not guaranteed.

Estimated survival probability — what the data says

These figures are population-level estimates drawn from peer-reviewed trauma literature. They cannot predict what any individual patient will experience — they describe what happens across large groups of patients with comparable mechanisms and injuries. They are included here to give an honest sense of the statistical weight of what Carolyn survived.

1. Pedestrian fatality risk at 35–45 mph impact speed

Data from NHTSA, the World Health Organization, and multiple peer-reviewed studies consistently show that pedestrians struck at 35–40 mph face a fatality risk of approximately 45–60%. At 45 mph, this rises to approximately 70–80%. These figures are for standard pedestrian-vehicle collisions. Carolyn was not in a standard scenario — she was in the direct compression zone between a moving vehicle and a fixed pump structure, which concentrates force rather than allowing the body to be projected away. This geometry is materially worse than the population average on which these figures are based. A reasonable upward adjustment for compression zone mechanics would place her mechanism-alone fatality risk closer to 55–70%.

2. In-hospital mortality for her injury severity profile

Using the Injury Severity Score (ISS) framework, Carolyn's injuries — unstable pelvic ring fractures (AIS 4–5), sacral fracture with neurological deficit (AIS 3–4), and hemorrhagic shock — place her estimated ISS in the range of 29–40. National Trauma Data Bank (NTDB) outcomes data for patients with ISS in the 25–40 range — the published band that encompasses Carolyn's estimated ISS of 29–40 — shows in-hospital mortality of approximately 15–25% at Level 1 trauma centers. Patients presenting with hemorrhagic shock and hemoglobin nadir below 7 g/dL face higher mortality within that band. Her specific combination — unstable pelvic ring injury with Class III–IV hemorrhagic shock — carries a reported mortality rate of 20–40% in published pelvic trauma literature, even at major centers.

3. Composite probability of surviving to day 8

Combining the mechanism (compression zone at 35–45 mph) with the injury severity (critical polytrauma, hemorrhagic shock, ISS ~29–40) and the care received (Level 1 trauma center with immediate surgical intervention), a reasonable composite estimate of survival probability — being alive at day 8 — is approximately 30–50%. The most defensible single-figure estimate, accounting for all three factors, is roughly 1-in-3 to 1-in-2. Stated plainly: across a population of patients who experienced this exact combination of mechanism, injury, and hemorrhagic crisis, more patients would be expected not to survive than to reach day 8 in a recovery phase.

What improved her odds

Several factors pulled her survival probability toward the better end of the range. Age and baseline health: patients under 45 with no comorbidities have significantly better survival than the population average for equivalent ISS. Rapid response: Kirkland Fire & Rescue were on scene quickly, limiting the pre-hospital hemorrhage window. Harborview specifically: Level 1 centers with dedicated pelvic trauma expertise have meaningfully better outcomes than general hospitals for this injury pattern — one study found a 30% reduction in mortality for unstable pelvic fractures treated at specialized centers versus community hospitals. Speed of definitive surgery: time-to-OR for hemorrhagic pelvic trauma directly correlates with survival; she was in the operating room on April 6 with damage control achieved. None of these factors make survival certain. Together, they represent the best possible version of an extremely dangerous situation.

Conditional Survival — Odds at Day 8
The odds have shifted fundamentally. The acute mortality chapter is closing.
The survival probability documented above reflects the danger at the moment of impact and through the acute surgical phase. Those numbers no longer apply. Medical literature establishes the concept of conditional survival — the probability of surviving given that you have already survived to a given point. For major trauma, the vast majority of deaths occur in the first 24–72 hours. Reaching day 8 hemodynamically stable, without a ventilator, with normalizing labs, is a clinically distinct milestone. Carolyn has cleared it.
Estimated survival probability — day 0 through day 8 and beyond
Conditional survival: at each point on the curve, the value represents the estimated probability of continuing to survive, given that Carolyn has already survived to that moment. These values rise as each danger is cleared. This is distinct from the 30–50% unconditional probability stated in the text above, which was the estimated chance — from day 0 — of reaching day 8 at all. Both figures are valid; they measure different things. Based on NTDB conditional survival data and pelvic trauma literature. Population-level estimates.
Phase cleared — Scene & arrival
Uncontrolled hemorrhage window
The mechanism-based 55–70% fatality risk lived in the immediate post-impact phase — uncontrolled pelvic hemorrhage before surgical control was achieved. This is where most pedestrian fatalities at this speed and mechanism occur, within minutes to hours of impact. Carolyn survived this phase. It is behind her.
Phase cleared — April 6 surgery
Hemorrhagic shock during ORIF
The second major mortality spike in trauma occurs intraoperatively and in the 12–24 hours following major surgery in a hemorrhagic shock patient. Her hemoglobin was 6.8 and hematocrit 19% going into 4 hours and 21 minutes of pelvic reconstruction. She came out the other side. The pharmacological intensity of that procedure — vasopressors, tranexamic acid, calcium chloride, four blood products — reflects how close the margin was. She cleared it.
Phase cleared — Days 1–5
Early post-operative collapse window
The 48–72 hours following major trauma surgery is when physiologic collapse, acute cardiac events, and early-onset sepsis claim most remaining in-hospital deaths. By day 4–5 post-injury, Carolyn's lactate had normalized, troponins were clean across three draws, WBC had settled to normal range, and she was stable enough for a second major surgery on April 8. She moved through this window without complication.
Current position — Day 8
Conditional survival: ~92–97%
Trauma patients with comparable injury severity (ISS 29–40) who reach day 7–8 hemodynamically stable, without mechanical ventilation, with normalizing labs and no active infection, have an estimated in-hospital mortality rate of approximately 3–8% based on National Trauma Data Bank conditional survival data. Stated positively: she has roughly a 92–97% probability of surviving the hospitalization from this point forward.
What changed between admission and now
At admission, the primary question was survival. The injury severity, hemorrhagic shock, and mechanism placed her in genuine mortal danger. By day 8, all acute mortality triggers have been surgically addressed, her coagulation is intact, her cardiac workup is clean, her infection surveillance is negative, and her vital signs are stable. The conditional survival framework reflects this: each day of stability past the acute phase further narrows the remaining mortality risk. The 3–8% figure is not complacency — the risks below are real — but it represents a fundamentally different situation than admission day.
Remaining mortality risks — must be actively managed
Pulmonary embolism (PE) is the leading cause of late in-hospital and early post-discharge death in major trauma recovery. Pelvic fractures plus prolonged immobility represent the highest-risk combination for DVT/PE. This risk remains elevated for weeks. Anticoagulation management is not optional — it is essential. Deep surgical site infection around pelvic or spinal hardware, if it develops, is serious and potentially life-threatening if it progresses to sepsis. Monitor for fever, wound changes, rising WBC. Secondary neurological deterioration is a lower-probability but real risk given the spinal injury pattern.
The framing has shifted
The relevant clinical question is no longer survival versus death. It is the quality and completeness of recovery — the trajectory of L5/S1 nerve regeneration, functional outcome at 12 and 24 months, and what her life looks like on the other side of this. That is where the meaningful uncertainty now lives. The focus now shifts from survival to recovery.
Recovery Outlook
Overview — a long road, but a realistic one
Carolyn's stated goal of walking in approximately six months is medically realistic, but it is an ambitious target that hinges heavily on one factor above all others: how fully the L5 and S1 nerve roots recover following the sacral fractures and spinal fusion. The bone healing, the pelvic hardware, and the anemia will all resolve on predictable timelines. The nerve recovery will not follow a predictable schedule, and its degree of completeness will determine more about her functional outcome than any other single variable. Everything below should be read with that in mind.
Phase 1 — Now through ~Week 6
Acute hospital recovery
Hemoglobin stabilization and anemia correction. Pain management transition from IV to oral. Wound healing (pelvis, spine, scalp, jaw). DVT prophylaxis critical — prolonged immobility + pelvic trauma is very high clot risk. Bowel and bladder function monitoring (S1 nerve involvement). No weight-bearing on pelvis. Flat bed rest with careful positioning. Monitoring for infection at surgical sites and in blood. Discharge to inpatient rehab facility likely at 2–4 weeks.
Phase 2 — Weeks 6–12
Early rehabilitation
Transition to inpatient or intensive outpatient rehab. Begin partial weight-bearing on pelvis as authorized by Dr. Kleweno — typically around 8–10 weeks for this fracture pattern. Spinal fusion hardware stabilizing but bone not yet consolidated. Physical therapy begins in earnest: bed mobility, transfers, upper body strengthening, gradual sitting tolerance. Right ankle fracture likely cleared for weight-bearing by this phase. Jaw healing progressing — soft diet continues. First major surgical follow-up imaging (X-ray / CT) to assess hardware and fracture healing.
Phase 3 — Months 3–6
Active rehabilitation — the walking goal
Progressive weight-bearing toward full load. Pelvic bone healing consolidating around months 3–4 if hardware holds and no complications. Walking with assistive device (walker → cane) realistic in this window if nerve recovery is on track. Intensive PT for gait training, hip abductor strengthening, core stability. Spinal fusion maturing but not complete — no high-impact activity. Nerve recovery: L5/S1 roots may show meaningful improvement in this phase, or may plateau. Functional milestones assessed. Return to driving, basic independence goals. Psychological and emotional recovery in active phase.
Phase 4 — Months 6–18
Long-term functional recovery
Spinal fusion fully consolidated by ~12 months. Continued nerve recovery — L5/S1 roots can continue improving for 12–18 months post-injury; improvement after 18 months is less likely. Return to more demanding activities depends entirely on nerve and pelvic stability outcomes. Hardware may need removal if symptomatic (separate elective procedure). Ongoing PT, pain management, and monitoring. Realistic assessment of permanent functional limitations, if any, possible by 12–18 months.
The critical wildcard: L5 and S1 nerve recovery

The L5 nerve root controls the muscles that lift the foot (dorsiflexion) and the sensation along the outer shin and top of the foot. The S1 nerve root controls push-off strength when walking (plantar flexion), the calf muscles, and sensation along the outer foot and heel. New deficits in both were confirmed on April 8, prompting emergency surgery.

The spinal fusion was performed to decompress these roots and prevent further damage — it does not guarantee recovery of function already lost. Nerve tissue heals slowly and incompletely. The range of outcomes spans from full functional recovery (possible, especially given her age and the speed of surgical decompression) to partial recovery (most common — some weakness or sensory loss remains) to minimal recovery (less likely given how quickly she was treated, but possible).

What to watch for: Foot drop (inability to lift the toes when walking) is the most functionally significant risk from L5 involvement — it directly affects gait and may require an ankle-foot orthosis (brace). Reduced push-off strength from S1 involvement affects walking speed and stair climbing. Both can be significantly compensated for with therapy and adaptive equipment even if nerve recovery is incomplete. Neurological assessment at 3, 6, and 12 months post-injury will be the clearest indicators of trajectory.

Deep vein thrombosis / PE
Highest-priority ongoing risk. Pelvic fracture + prolonged immobility = very high DVT risk. Pulmonary embolism is a leading cause of death in trauma recovery. Anticoagulation management and compression devices are essential throughout early phases. Must be monitored closely at every stage.
Hardware failure or malunion
The pelvic plate, screws, and SI joint fixation must hold while the bone heals. Hardware failure before consolidation would require revision surgery. Serial X-rays at follow-up appointments are essential. Weight-bearing restrictions in Phase 1–2 exist specifically to protect the construct.
Surgical site infection
Two major open surgical wounds (pelvis and spine) in an immunologically stressed patient. Deep infection around hardware is serious and can require hardware removal. Watch for fever, wound drainage, elevated WBC, or CRP at follow-ups.
Post-traumatic arthritis
Fractures extending into the left hip joint and SI joint carry meaningful risk of post-traumatic arthritis over years. Not an acute concern, but a long-term reality to plan for. May affect activity level and require management years down the line.
Chronic pain
Persistent pelvic, lumbar, and sacral pain is common after injuries of this severity, even with successful hardware fixation and bone healing. Pain management — including non-opioid strategies, physical therapy, and potentially pain specialist involvement — will be an ongoing part of recovery.
Psychological / PTSD
A life-threatening traumatic accident, prolonged hospitalization, major functional impairment, and dependency during recovery are all significant psychological stressors — for Carolyn and for the family. PTSD, depression, and anxiety are common sequelae of critical trauma. Proactive engagement with mental health support is as important as physical rehabilitation. Mira witnessed the accident and should also be assessed for trauma response.
Athletic Prognosis — Return to Sport & Activity
The nerve recovery is the master variable — but the activity spectrum is wide
The question of whether Carolyn will return to running, hiking, swimming, cycling, or backpacking cannot be answered definitively today. The structural reconstruction — the pelvic hardware, the spinal fusion, the ankle — all have a reasonable prognosis. The degree of L5 and S1 nerve recovery is the single variable that determines the ceiling. Crucially, different activities make very different demands on those nerve roots. Swimming barely requires them. Backpacking requires everything. The analysis below is specific to each activity.
Swimming — best prognosis
Most likely activity to return to fully
Swimming places almost no demand on L5/S1 nerve function, imposes no weight-bearing load on the pelvis or fusion, and is upper-body dominant in its early phases. It is the activity least constrained by her specific injury pattern. Light pool-based movement can begin as early as 3–4 months once surgical wounds are fully healed and the care team approves water exposure. Lap swimming with full stroke mechanics is realistic by 6–9 months even with partial nerve recovery. Swimming is the activity most likely to return fully and earliest, and may be an important component of early physical rehabilitation.
Cycling — strong prognosis
Low-impact, good early return potential
Stationary cycling can begin as early as 3–5 months and is a cornerstone of early pelvic and spinal rehabilitation — it restores range of motion and cardiovascular fitness without axial impact. Road cycling on flat terrain follows at approximately 9–15 months pending hardware stability. L5/S1 involvement affects foot positioning on pedals (dorsiflexion) but adaptations such as clipless pedals and modified cleat angles can compensate effectively for residual deficits. The pelvic fusion is not a contraindication to cycling. Trail or mountain biking — with its vibration and unpredictable loads — would need to wait until 18–24+ months and confirmed spinal fusion consolidation.
Hiking — good prognosis
Achievable even with partial nerve recovery
Hiking on moderate terrain with trekking poles is achievable across a wide range of neurological outcomes. Poles compensate significantly for reduced push-off strength (S1) and altered gait mechanics. Flat to moderate trail hiking is realistic at 9–15 months if nerve recovery is on track. Steeper, more technical terrain follows at 15–24 months. Even with an ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) for residual foot drop, hiking with poles remains well within reach. The L5–ilium fusion limits trunk rotation and hip drive on steep ascents, but trekking poles compensate. Of the land-based activities, hiking carries the most optimistic prognosis for meaningful return even in partial-recovery scenarios.
Running — conditional prognosis
Possible, but nerve-dependent
Running requires full L5 dorsiflexion (foot clearance) and full S1 plantar flexion (push-off power). Neither can be meaningfully substituted or adapted around. If both roots recover fully or near-fully, running is a legitimate goal at 18–24 months. The L5–ilium fusion constrains pelvic rotation and will alter stride mechanics regardless of nerve outcome. If there is residual foot drop, running becomes very difficult. The 6–12 month neurological trajectory will answer this question more definitively than anything knowable today. Running is the most nerve-dependent activity on this list and therefore carries the widest range of possible outcomes.
Backpacking — most challenging
Possible long-term; significant constraints
Backpacking combines the demands of hiking with axial load-bearing (pack weight transmitted directly through the lumbar spine and fusion), prolonged exertion, uneven terrain, and remote conditions that limit access to care. The L5–ilium fusion is the primary structural constraint — load-bearing on an instrumented fusion requires confirmed consolidation at 12+ months, and heavy packs are typically discouraged before 18–24 months. L5/S1 nerve recovery again determines the functional gait component. Realistic timeline for light backpacking (modest pack, well-graded terrain): 24–36 months pending fusion consolidation and nerve recovery. Multi-day backcountry trips require reassessment at 24–36 months. This is the longest timeline but not an unrealistic long-term goal.
Earliest possible timeline
Carolyn would not be cleared to attempt any running motion before 12 months post-injury at the earliest. The spinal fusion must be fully consolidated, the pelvic hardware must be proven stable under full loading, and the neurological picture must be sufficiently clear. Attempting to run before that risks hardware failure and re-injury. The optimistic path — if nerve recovery cooperates — is a first attempt at slow jogging in the 18–24 month window from April 2026.
What months 6–12 will reveal
Nerve recovery tends to show its trajectory within the first 6–12 months. By approximately month 6–9, it will be substantially clearer whether she is regaining dorsiflexion (L5) and push-off strength (S1), whether foot drop is resolving or persisting, and how her gait is developing under therapy. That assessment — not anything knowable today — will give the most grounded answer to whether running is a realistic goal or whether the focus should shift to optimizing walking and other activities.
If nerve recovery is incomplete
Residual foot drop or S1 weakness does not mean an active life is out of reach. An ankle-foot orthosis (AFO) brace can compensate meaningfully for foot drop during walking and light activity. Many people with partial nerve deficits following similar injuries return to hiking, cycling, swimming, and other demanding activities — just not running in the traditional sense. The goal of an active, physical life remains realistic across a wide range of neurological outcomes.
Recommended milestones to track
Neurological assessment at 3, 6, 9, and 12 months post-injury to document L5/S1 motor and sensory recovery. EMG/nerve conduction study at 3–6 months to quantify nerve regeneration. Gait analysis at 6 months to assess mechanics and identify compensatory patterns early. Pelvic and spinal imaging at 6 and 12 months to confirm healing and hardware integrity before any return-to-impact discussion begins.
Activity return timeline summary
3–4 months: Pool movement, stationary cycling — pending wound healing clearance
6–9 months: Lap swimming, flat-terrain cycling
9–15 months: Road cycling, moderate trail hiking with poles
12 months: Earliest possible clearance for any running motion — fusion must be consolidated, hardware proven stable, neurological picture clear
18–24 months: Road running (if nerve recovery supports it), technical hiking, light trail cycling
24–36 months: Light backpacking pending fusion consolidation and nerve recovery assessment
All timelines conditional on ongoing neurological trajectory. Swimming and cycling carry the most optimistic outlook; running and backpacking depend most heavily on L5/S1 nerve recovery.
